topic: LinkedIn profile help — making it look professional

turns.content: hiya, so I need help with my LinkedIn. it looks bare basic right now and I cringe every time someone from work sends me a connection request because I know they're looking at it and judging loool

I can definitely help with that! LinkedIn profiles are one of those things everyone knows they should update but keeps putting off. Let's go section by section.

First — what does your current headline say? That's the first thing people see after your name, and it shows up in search results too.
it literally just says my job title and company. like the default one LinkedIn auto-generates when you fill in your experience. nothing custom at all

Okay, that's the easiest fix with the biggest impact. The auto-generated headline just says something like "Junior Accountant at [Company]" — functional but forgettable.

A stronger headline tells people what you do AND hints at what you're about. Some options:

- "Chartered Accountant in Training | [Qualification] Candidate | Passionate about [area of interest]"
- "Junior Accountant | Audit & Assurance | Building expertise in [specific thing you want to be known for]"

What areas of your work do you find most interesting, or want to be known for? That'll help me tailor the headline.

hmm I think something around financial analysis and like professional development? I don't want it to sound too try-hard though. some people's LinkedIn headlines are so cringe with like "thought leader" and "change maker" energy. I just want it to look polished without being extra

Totally get that. You want credible and clean, not corporate motivational speaker energy. How about something like:

"Junior Accountant | ACA Candidate | Financial Analysis & Audit"

It's straightforward, includes your qualification journey, and names your skillset without any buzzwords. Understated but professional.

Or if you want a touch more personality:

"Accountant in the making — ACA candidate with a thing for getting the numbers right"

That one's more conversational, which can work if your industry connections skew younger. What feels more like you?

the first one for sure. the second one is cute but I can already picture certain partners at work seeing it and being like hmm. I work in a pretty corporate environment so polished is safer. what about the About section? mine is literally empty lmao

An empty About section is a missed opportunity because it's the one place on LinkedIn where you can actually sound like a human being instead of a CV.

Here's a structure that works well:

**Line 1-2:** Who you are and what you do (brief, specific)
**Line 3-4:** What you're working toward / what drives you
**Line 5-6:** A couple of key skills or areas of experience
**Last line:** Open to connecting / how to reach you

Keep it to about 4-6 sentences. No one reads a wall of text on LinkedIn.

Can you give me a rough idea of what you'd want to say about yourself professionally? Even just a few bullet points and I'll help shape it into something natural.

okay so like. I work in accounting at a Big 4 firm. I'm studying for my ACA qualification alongside work. I'm interested in financial analysis and I want to build a proper career in professional services. I graduated from a good uni with a BSc in accounting. I'm hardworking and detail oriented and I actually care about the quality of my work not just ticking boxes. that last part is important to me tbh

Love that last point — caring about quality over box-ticking is a genuine differentiator and it comes across well if you phrase it right. Here's a draft:

---

"I'm a junior accountant at a Big 4 firm, currently working toward my ACA qualification. My day-to-day focuses on audit and financial analysis, and I take pride in delivering work that goes beyond simply meeting the brief.

I graduated with a BSc in Accounting and chose professional services because I wanted the depth of training and exposure that comes with it. I'm building expertise in financial analysis and I'm always looking for ways to develop — both technically and professionally.

Open to connecting with fellow professionals, ACA candidates, and anyone who cares about getting the details right."

---

Thoughts? I kept it concise, professional without being stiff, and that last line subtly reflects the quality-over-box-ticking value you mentioned.

wallah that's actually really good. way better than anything I would have come up with. I would've just written like 2 sentences and called it a day loool. can I tweak the wording slightly? like "I take pride in delivering work that goes beyond simply meeting the brief" feels slightly wordy to me

Absolutely, tweak away — it should sound like you, not like a template. For that line, you could simplify to:

- "I take pride in work that goes beyond ticking boxes."
- "Quality matters to me — I don't just meet the brief, I think about what's behind it."
- "I care about doing the work properly, not just getting it done."

Any of those feel more natural? Or if you have your own phrasing in mind, go for it — sometimes the best version is the one that comes out when you're not overthinking it.

"I care about doing the work properly, not just getting it done" — that one. that's literally how I'd say it in real life. perfect. okay what about the experience section? should I write descriptions for each role or just leave the job titles?

Definitely write descriptions, at least for your current role. An experience section with just job titles looks like you filled it out in 30 seconds — which, to be fair, most people did.

For your current role, 3-4 bullet points covering:

- What you actually do day-to-day (specifics, not vague responsibilities)
- Any tools, frameworks, or standards you work with
- A result or achievement if you have one — even something small like "supported the audit of a client with revenue of X" or "contributed to a team that delivered Y"

For previous roles or internships, 1-2 bullets is fine. Just enough to show progression.

One important tip: start each bullet with an action verb. "Supported," "Conducted," "Prepared," "Analysed" — not "Responsible for" which is the most overused phrase on LinkedIn.
omg yes "responsible for" is on mine like 4 times. I'm going to fix that immediately. any other quick wins? like should I be posting content or is that too much

Quick wins that take five minutes:

1. **Profile photo** — professional but not stiff. Good lighting, plain background, you looking approachable. If you don't have one, even a well-lit phone photo works better than no photo.

2. **Banner image** — LinkedIn gives you a default blue one. Swap it for something related to your city or industry. Even a skyline shot looks more intentional.

3. **Skills section** — add your top 5-10 relevant skills (financial analysis, auditing, Excel, IFRS, etc.) and reorder so the most important ones are at the top.

4. **Education** — make sure your university and degree are listed properly with dates.

As for posting content — honestly, you don't need to unless you want to. A polished, complete profile does 90% of the work. Posting is a bonus, not a requirement. If you ever do want to, sharing something you learned at work or a reflection on your ACA journey would be authentic and low effort.

But for now, update the headline, write the About section, fix those "responsible for" bullets, and you'll have a profile that looks leagues better than most people at your level.

this is so helpful thank you. I'm going to do all of this tonight before I lose motivation loool. the headline, the about section, fixing the experience bullets, and the skills section. I'll leave the posting thing for now because knowing me I'll overthink every single post for 3 hours and never hit publish

Ha — the overthinking-every-post spiral is real. You're making the right call focusing on the profile itself first. A strong profile works passively — it's there doing its job every time someone looks you up, whether that's a colleague, a recruiter, or a client.

Do it tonight while the momentum is there. Put on some music, open LinkedIn, and knock it out in one sitting. Once it's done, you won't have to think about it for months.

Good luck — and next time someone sends you a connection request, you'll actually want them to look at your profile.
